perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall pay Club the Annual Fee for each Contract Year of this Agreement in six (6) equal installments, each due on or prior to the 1st of each month between June and November of the applicable Contract Year.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall pay Club the Annual Fee for each Contract Year of this Agreement in installments as deemed appropriate by the Club, to be paid between June and November of the applicable Contract Year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms from fixed installments to installments 'as deemed appropriate by the Club' introduces ambiguity and contradicts standard contract law principles regarding payment certainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 672.305 addresses open price terms in contracts for the sale of goods. While this sponsorship agreement isn't directly a sale of goods, the principle that price (or payment terms) must be determined in good faith and with commercial reasonableness is applicable. The modified text gives the Club unilateral discretion to determine installment amounts, which may not be considered good faith or commercially reasonable, thus contradicting the spirit of this statute regarding fair dealing in contractual obligations.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 305 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 305 Open price term. F.S. 672.305 672.305 Open price term. — (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if: (a) Nothing is said as to price; or (b) The price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) The price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for her or him to fix in good faith. (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at her or his option treat the contract as canceled or herself or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract. In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 560, ch. 97-102. Note. — s. 2-305, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text gives the Club the unilateral right to decide payment amounts, contrasting with the original text that specified fixed, equal installments. The Law Explanation points to Florida Statutes § 672.305, which covers open price terms and requires prices to be determined in good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing F.S. 672.305, states that even if the price is not settled, it should be a 'reasonable price at the time for delivery'. By allowing the Club to determine the installment amounts arbitrarily, the modified text potentially removes the 'good faith' and 'reasonable price' requirements, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be considered given when delivered personally or by electronic mail (confirmed by one of the other permissible methods of giving notice hereunder), one business day after being sent by a nationally recognized overnight courier, or three business days after being mailed by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid and return receipt requested, to the parties at the following addresses (or at such other address as a party may specify by notice to the other):
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice or other communication under this Agreement may be delivered through methods the Club deems sufficient. The Club's determination on receipt of the message shall be final.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the requirement for written notice and replaces it with a subjective standard of 'methods the Club deems sufficient,' contradicting standard legal requirements for notice in contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 671.202 - Notice; knowledge; or a notice or notification received by an organization
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 671.202 defines when a person or organization has 'notice' or 'knowledge' of a fact. By allowing the Club to unilaterally determine the sufficiency of notice, the changed text circumvents the objective standards for notice established by this statute. The statute requires reasonable time for notification, which is not guaranteed here, thus creating a contradiction.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 671 > Section 202 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 671 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 202 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. F.S. 671.202 671.202 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. — A document in due form purporting to be a bill of lading, policy or certificate of insurance, official weigher’s or inspector’s certificate, consular invoice, or any other document authorized or required by the contract to be issued by a third party is prima facie evidence of its own authenticity and genuineness and of the facts stated in the document by the third party. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254; s. 9, ch. 2007-134. Note. — s. 1-202, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Club to determine the sufficiency of notice, which contradicts the standard legal requirements for notice. The original text specifies methods for delivering notice, whereas the changed text gives the club unilateral control. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to Florida Statute 671.202, which, although it discusses prima facie evidence by third-party documents, indirectly supports the idea that notice should adhere to some verifiable standard. The Law Explanation argues the change circumvents objective standards and doesn't guarantee reasonable notification, which, combined with the perturbation explanation defining it as misaligned terminology, indicates a significant deviation from legally recognized definitions, thus making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The exact size and location of the Stands shall be determined by Club. The exact design of the Stands shall be mutually agreed upon between Sponsor and Club. Sponsor may display Signage displaying a Sponsor Mark in and/or on the Stand. Sponsor shall be responsible for all costs associated with the branding and Advertising in respect of the Stand.
perturbation.changed_text: The size, location and design of the Stands shall be determined solely by Club. Signage and advertising shall be at Club's sole discretion. Sponsor may or may not be responsible for costs, as determined by Club.
perturbation.explanation: This modification alters the terms of control and responsibility for the concession stands. The original text allows for mutual agreement on design, but the modification grants the Club unilateral control over all aspects, creating uncertainty and potential breach of contract issues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.306 - Output, requirements and exclusive dealings
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 672.306 implies a duty of good faith and commercial reasonableness in contracts involving output or requirements. While not directly applicable, the principle extends to exclusive dealings. By giving the Club complete control over the stands, signage, and costs, the modified text creates an imbalance that could violate the implied duty of good faith, especially if the Club acts unreasonably in a way that harms the Sponsor's ability to benefit from the agreement. This creates a legal contradiction as the modified terms could be deemed unconscionable.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 672 > Section 306 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 672 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE: SALES Entire Chapter SECTION 306 Output, requirements, and exclusive dealings. F.S. 672.306 672.306 Output, requirements, and exclusive dealings. — (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith, except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale. History. — s. 1, ch. 65-254. Note. — s. 2-306, U.C.C.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of good faith implied in contracts, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. The original text ensured a mutual agreement between the Sponsor and Club regarding the design of the stands. The changed text gives the Club sole discretion over the stands, signage, and costs, potentially harming the Sponsor's ability to benefit from the agreement. This shift in control contradicts the good faith obligation, as it allows the Club to act unreasonably, potentially nullifying the sponsor's benefits, thus creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition, by altering legally recognized definitions of fairness.
